content:
# Exodus Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~35r–36r (PDF pp. 68–69); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 48–49 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_008_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_008_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Second Plague: Frogs (vv. 1–15)

**1** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Go to Pharaoh and tell him: This is what YHWH says — Let my people go, so they may serve me.

**2** If you refuse to let them go, I am about to strike your entire territory with frogs.

**3** The Nile will swarm with frogs. They will come up and invade your palace, your bedroom, your very bed — and the houses of your officials, and your people's homes — even into your ovens and your kneading bowls.

**4** The frogs will crawl over you, over your people, and over all your officials."

**5** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Tell Aaron: Stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, the canals, and the pools, and bring the frogs up over the land of Egypt."

**6** Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt.

**7** But the magicians did the same thing with their secret arts — they too brought frogs up over the land of Egypt.

**8** Then Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron and said, "Plead with YHWH to take the frogs away from me and from my people, and I will let the people go to sacrifice to YHWH."

**9** Moses said to Pharaoh, "The honor is yours — set the time! When shall I plead for you, for your officials, and for your people, to cut off the frogs from you and your houses, so that they remain only in the Nile?"

**10** He said, "Tomorrow." And Moses said, "As you say — so that you will know there is no one like YHWH our God.

**11** The frogs will retreat from you, from your houses, from your officials, and from your people. They will remain only in the Nile."

**12** Moses and Aaron left Pharaoh, and Moses cried out to YHWH about the frogs he had brought against Pharaoh.

**13** YHWH did as Moses asked: the frogs died — out of the houses, out of the courtyards, out of the fields.

**14** They piled them in heap after heap, and the land reeked.

**15** But when Pharaoh saw that relief had come, he made his heart heavy and did not listen to them — just as YHWH had said.

### The Third Plague: Gnats (vv. 16–19)

**16** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Tell Aaron: Stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the ground, and it will become gnats throughout the entire land of Egypt."

**17** They did it. Aaron stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust of the ground, and the gnats came — on people and on animals. All the dust of the ground became gnats throughout the entire land of Egypt.

**18** The magicians tried to produce gnats with their secret arts, but they could not. The gnats were on people and on animals.

**19** The magicians said to Pharaoh, "This is the finger of God!" But Pharaoh's heart grew rigid, and he did not listen to them — just as YHWH had said.

### The Fourth Plague: Swarms (vv. 20–32)

**20** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Get up early in the morning and station yourself before Pharaoh — he will be going out to the water — and say to him: This is what YHWH says — Let my people go, so they may serve me.

**21** Because if you will not let my people go, I am about to send swarms of insects on you, on your officials, on your people, and into your houses. The houses of Egypt will be filled with the swarms, and even the ground they stand on.

**22** But on that day I will set apart the land of Goshen, where my people dwell — no swarms will be there — so that you will know that I, YHWH, am present in this land.

**23** I will put a division between my people and your people. By tomorrow this sign will come."

**24** And YHWH did it. A massive swarm of insects came into Pharaoh's palace and into the houses of his officials. Throughout all of Egypt the land was devastated by the swarms.

**25** Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron and said, "Go — sacrifice to your God, but do it here in the land."

**26** Moses said, "That is impossible, because what we sacrifice to YHWH our God is detestable to the Egyptians. If we slaughter what the Egyptians consider sacred right before their eyes, will they not stone us?

**27** We must go a three-day journey into the wilderness and sacrifice to YHWH our God, exactly as he tells us."

**28** Pharaoh said, "I will let you go to sacrifice to YHWH your God in the wilderness — only do not go far! Now plead for me."

**29** Moses said, "The moment I leave you I will plead with YHWH, and tomorrow the swarms will depart from Pharaoh, from his officials, and from his people. But Pharaoh must not deal deceptively again by refusing to let the people go to sacrifice to YHWH."

**30** Moses left Pharaoh and pleaded with YHWH.

**31** And YHWH did as Moses asked: he removed the swarms from Pharaoh, from his officials, and from his people. Not one remained.

**32** But Pharaoh made his heart heavy this time also, and he did not let the people go.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Versification note:** The Hebrew MT begins this chapter at 7:26 (English 8:1). Throughout the plague narratives, the MT and English Bibles are offset by several verses. PNEUMA follows English versification (32 verses) for accessibility, with MT references noted where relevant.

**v.1 — "Let my people go, so they may serve me" (Heb. *shallach et-ammi veya'avduni*, שַׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־עַמִּ֖י וְיַעַבְדֻֽנִי):** This demand, first issued in 5:1, now becomes the refrain of the plague cycle. The verb *avad* (עבד) means "to serve, to work, to worship" — it is the same verb used for Israel's slave labor in Egypt (*avodah*, עבודה). The demand is not merely "release" but *transfer of service*: Israel will stop serving Pharaoh and start serving YHWH. The wordplay is intentional and runs through the entire exodus narrative. Pharaoh is being told that his labor force belongs to someone else, and the work they were made for is worship, not bricks.

**v.2 — "frogs" (Heb. *tsefarde'im*, צְפַרְדְּעִ֑ים):** The frog (*tsefardea'*) appears in the Hebrew Bible almost exclusively in connection with this plague (here and Ps. 78:45, 105:30). The Egyptian goddess Heqet, depicted with a frog's head, was associated with fertility and childbirth — the very blessings the Israelites were experiencing in abundance (1:7). The plague turns Egypt's fertility symbol into an instrument of revulsion. The Vaticanus uses *batrachous* (βατράχους), the standard Greek word. PNEUMA retains the plain English "frogs" because the theological irony operates at the cultural level, not the linguistic level.

**v.3 — "your bedroom, your very bed" (Heb. *bachader mishkavekha ve'al mittatekha*, בַּחֲדַ֤ר מִשְׁכָּֽבְךָ֙ וְעַל־מִטָּתֶ֔ךָ):** The invasion is described in concentric rings of intimacy: territory, palace, bedroom, bed. Pharaoh, who commands the most powerful empire on earth, will not be able to keep frogs out of his own sheets. The humiliation is the point — the creature sacred to Heqet is now an intolerable pest. The detail about ovens (*tannur*, תַּנּוּר) and kneading bowls (*mish'eret*, מִשְׁאֶרֶת) means that food preparation itself is contaminated. Daily life becomes impossible.

**v.7 — The magicians replicate the frogs:** As with the blood plague, the magicians' replication is absurd. Egypt is already drowning in frogs. The magicians' response is to produce *more* frogs. They can imitate the plague but not reverse it — they can add to the crisis but not solve it. This is the second time the narrator makes this devastating observation. The pattern establishes a principle: Egypt's power can only amplify destruction, never bring healing. The Vaticanus preserves this with *epoiēsan hōsautōs* (ἐποίησαν ὡσαύτως), "they did likewise."

**v.8 — Pharaoh's first prayer request (Heb. *ha'tiru el-YHWH*, הַעְתִּ֣ירוּ אֶל־יְהוָ֗ה):** This is the first time Pharaoh asks for intercession, and the verb is striking. *Ha'tiru* (from *atar*, עתר) means "to plead, to entreat" — it is an intensive form suggesting urgent, persistent prayer. Pharaoh, who asked "Who is YHWH?" in 5:2, now implicitly acknowledges YHWH's power by requesting prayer to him. He does not ask his own gods or his own magicians to remove the frogs — he asks Moses to plead with YHWH. This is a crack in the theological edifice, though Pharaoh will seal it again as soon as the pressure lifts.

**v.9 — "The honor is yours — set the time!" (Heb. *hitpa'er alai*, הִתְפָּאֵ֣ר עָלַ֔י):** This extraordinary phrase has puzzled translators. The verb *hitpa'er* means "to glorify oneself, to boast, to take the honor." Moses is essentially saying to Pharaoh: "You choose the timing — the honor of deciding is yours." This is not deference but a demonstration of power. Moses is so confident in YHWH's ability that he lets Pharaoh name the deadline. It is an open challenge: pick any time, and YHWH will meet it. The Vaticanus renders this *taxai pros me* (τάξαι πρός με), "appoint for me [a time]," flattening the Hebrew's dramatic flair. PNEUMA captures the force: Moses is handing Pharaoh the initiative precisely to demonstrate that YHWH controls the outcome.

**v.10 — "Tomorrow" (Heb. *lemachar*, לְמָחָ֑ר):** Pharaoh says "tomorrow" — not "now," not "immediately." Why wait? The best explanation is that Pharaoh is testing: he wants to see if Moses (and YHWH) can deliver on a specific schedule. Removing the frogs instantly might be explained as coincidence or natural die-off. But removing them precisely *tomorrow*, on command, would demonstrate genuine supernatural control. Moses accepts the terms and names the purpose: "so that you will know there is no one like YHWH our God" (*ki-ein ka-YHWH Eloheinu*, כִּי־אֵ֖ין כַּיהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֵֽינוּ). The recognition formula appears again — the plagues are pedagogical.

**v.14 — "heap after heap" (Heb. *chamorim chamorim*, חֳמָרִ֣ם חֳמָרִ֑ם):** The Hebrew doubles the word *chomer* (חמר) — literally "heaps heaps," an intensification by repetition. The image is visceral: rotting piles of dead frogs across the landscape. The verb *tiv'ash* (תִּבְאַ֖שׁ), "it stank/reeked," is the same word used for the Nile in 7:18. The stench of divine judgment now pervades the entire land, not just the river. PNEUMA renders the doubling as "heap after heap" to preserve the Hebrew's repetitive emphasis.

**v.15 — "he made his heart heavy" (Heb. *vehakhbed et-libbo*, וְהַכְבֵּד֙ אֶת־לִבּ֔וֹ):** This is the *kaved* (כבד) hardening — "to make heavy." Critically, here it is in the Hiphil stem with Pharaoh as the agent: Pharaoh *caused his own heart to be heavy*. The moment relief comes (*harevacha*, הָרְוָחָ֔ה — "breathing room, respite"), Pharaoh actively chooses to dull his own heart. The narrator draws a direct line between comfort and moral regression: when the pressure lifts, Pharaoh reverts. This pattern — crisis produces concession, relief produces regression — will repeat throughout the plague cycle. The Vaticanus uses *ebarunen* (ἐβάρυνεν), "he made heavy," preserving the self-agency.

**v.16 — "Strike the dust of the ground" (Heb. *hakh et-afar ha'arets*, הַךְ֙ אֶת־עֲפַ֣ר הָאָ֔רֶץ):** The third plague comes without warning — no demand to Pharaoh, no ultimatum. This is the first unannounced plague, establishing a structural pattern: plagues 1-2-3 follow a warning-warning-no warning rhythm that repeats across the three cycles (1-2-3, 4-5-6, 7-8-9), with the tenth plague as the climax outside the pattern. The target is *afar* (עפר), "dust" — the very substance from which humanity was formed (Gen. 2:7) and to which it returns (Gen. 3:19). The dust of creation becomes the medium of plague.

**v.17 — "gnats" (Heb. *kinnim*, כִּנִּ֔ים):** The identification of *kinnim* is debated. Possibilities include gnats, lice, mosquitoes, or sand-flies. The LXX (Vaticanus) uses *skniphes* (σκνῖφες), typically understood as gnats or mosquitoes. The Targums render it as "lice." What matters narratively is the scale: "all the dust of the ground became gnats" (*kol-afar ha'arets hayah kinnim*, כָּל־עֲפַ֥ר הָאָ֛רֶץ הָיָ֥ה כִנִּ֖ם). This is creation-language in reverse — God transforms the elemental substance of the earth into a living plague. The parallel to Genesis 2:7 (dust becoming life) is unmistakable, but here the life that emerges from dust is tormenting rather than blessed. PNEUMA uses "gnats" as the most widely supported rendering.

**v.18 — The magicians' failure:** This is the theological turning point of the chapter — and arguably of the entire plague narrative's first movement. For the first time, the magicians *cannot replicate* the plague. The text states it with devastating brevity: *vayyasu khen hachartummim belatehem lehotsi et-hakinnim velo yakholu* — "the magicians tried with their secret arts to produce gnats, but they could not." Note the shift in the Hebrew word for "arts": here it is *belatehem* (בְּלָטֵיהֶ֛ם, from *lat*, "secrecy") rather than *belahatehem* (from *lahat*, "flame/blaze") used in 7:11 and 7:22. The flashy, dazzling arts have been replaced by mere hidden tricks — and even those fail. The magicians' power has been progressively diminished: they matched the serpent (but were swallowed), matched the blood (but made it worse), matched the frogs (but added to the problem), and now cannot match the gnats at all. The escalation of divine power meets the degradation of human counterfeit.

**v.19 — "The finger of God" (Heb. *etsba Elohim hi*, אֶצְבַּ֥ע אֱלֹהִ֖ים הִ֑וא):** The magicians' confession is one of the most theologically loaded phrases in Exodus. The "finger of God" (*etsba Elohim*) appears elsewhere in the Torah only at Exodus 31:18, where the tablets of the law are described as "written by the finger of God" — the same finger that creates gnats from dust writes the covenant on stone. The image suggests both precision and effortlessness: what overwhelms Egypt's entire magical establishment is, for God, merely a finger's work. The Vaticanus renders *daktulos theou estin touto* (δάκτυλος θεοῦ ἐστιν τοῦτο), "this is the finger of God." Jesus will later invoke this phrase in Luke 11:20 when casting out demons, explicitly linking his ministry to the exodus. The magicians are the ones who recognize what Pharaoh will not — that the power at work here is categorically different from anything human arts can produce. They will not appear again as active participants; their role in the narrative is finished. Pharaoh, however, ignores their testimony. The text says his heart "grew rigid" (*chazaq*) — the same verb as 7:13, 7:22. Even his own experts' confession cannot break through.

**v.20 — "Get up early in the morning" (Heb. *hashkem baboqer*, הַשְׁכֵּ֣ם בַּבֹּ֗קֶר):** The fourth plague opens the second cycle of three. Like the first cycle, it begins with a confrontation at the water. The command to rise early emphasizes urgency and divine initiative — YHWH sets the schedule. The demand is the same refrain: "Let my people go, so they may serve me." The Vaticanus has *orthrison to proi* (ὄρθρισον τὸ πρωΐ), "rise at dawn."

**v.21 — "swarms" (Heb. *he'arov*, הֶעָרֹ֗ב):** The word *arov* (עָרֹב) comes from the root *arav* (ערב), meaning "to mix, to mingle." It denotes a "mixture" — traditionally understood as a swarm of mixed insects, though some ancient interpreters (including certain rabbinic traditions) understood it as a mixture of wild beasts. The LXX (Vaticanus) uses *kunomyian* (κυνόμυιαν), "dog-fly," a specific insect identification. The Vulgate has *musca gravissima*, "very grievous fly." PNEUMA renders it "swarms of insects" to capture the Hebrew sense of overwhelming, mixed infestation while remaining readable. The key point is the totality: the swarms invade houses, cover the ground, and devastate the land.

**v.22 — "I will set apart the land of Goshen" (Heb. *vehifleiti vayom hahu et-erets Goshen*, וְהִפְלֵיתִ֣י בַיּ֣וֹם הַה֗וּא אֶת־אֶ֤רֶץ גֹּ֙שֶׁן֙):** This is the narrative's most significant theological development since the hardening announcement. For the first time, YHWH makes a *distinction* (*hiflah*, from *palah*, פלה — "to set apart, to distinguish, to make wonderful") between Israel and Egypt. The plagues are no longer universal — they now discriminate. This introduces the Goshen principle: God's people dwell in the same land as the oppressor, but judgment passes over them. This anticipates the Passover itself and establishes a theology of divine differentiation that runs through the rest of Scripture. The purpose clause is explicit: "so that you will know that I, YHWH, am present in this land" (*ki ani YHWH beqerev ha'arets*, כִּ֛י אֲנִ֥י יְהוָ֖ה בְּקֶ֥רֶב הָאָֽרֶץ). The word *beqerev* means "in the midst of, in the interior of" — YHWH is not a distant deity but is *present within* the land, actively governing what happens on the ground. The Vaticanus uses *paradoxasō* (παραδοξάσω), "I will make marvelous/extraordinary," suggesting the distinction itself is a wonder.

**v.23 — "a division" (Heb. *pedut*, פְדֻ֔ת):** The MT reads *pedut*, which derives from *padah* (פדה), "to redeem, to ransom." The concept is not merely "separation" but *redemption* — God puts a redemptive distinction between the two peoples. Some manuscripts and ancient versions read *peludah* (from *palad*, "to divide"), and the LXX seems to follow this reading. But the MT's *pedut* is theologically richer: the distinction between Israel and Egypt is itself an act of redemption. PNEUMA's rendering "division" captures the spatial reality while the notes preserve the redemptive undertone.

**v.25 — Pharaoh's first negotiation: "sacrifice here in the land":** Pharaoh begins his negotiation strategy. He concedes that the Israelites may sacrifice — but not leave. This is the first of four attempts to modify the demand: (1) sacrifice in Egypt (8:25), (2) don't go far (8:28), (3) leave women and children behind (10:11), (4) leave flocks behind (10:24). Each concession reveals what Pharaoh is truly unwilling to surrender: complete control over Israel's labor and movement. The pattern shows Pharaoh as a negotiator, not a penitent — he calculates the minimum concession needed to end each crisis.

**v.26 — "detestable to the Egyptians" (Heb. *to'avat Mitsrayim*, תּוֹעֲבַ֥ת מִצְרַ֖יִם):** Moses' counterargument is shrewd. The word *to'evah* (תועבה), "abomination, detestable thing," is the strongest term of religious revulsion in Hebrew. Moses claims that Israelite sacrifice would be an abomination to Egyptians — possibly because certain animals sacred to Egyptian gods (cattle associated with Apis or Hathor) would be slaughtered. Whether this is a genuine cultural concern or a tactical argument, it functions as an irrefutable reason why distance is necessary. The threat of stoning (*yisqelunu*, יִסְקְלֻֽנוּ) adds physical danger to the equation. The Vaticanus preserves this logic with *ta bdelugmata tōn Aiguptiōn* (τὰ βδελύγματα τῶν Αἰγυπτίων), "the abominations of the Egyptians."

**v.28 — Pharaoh's second concession: "only do not go far":** Pharaoh yields further but adds a condition: *raq harcheq lo-tarchiku lalechet* (רַ֛ק הַרְחֵ֥ק לֹא־תַרְחִ֖יקוּ לָלֶ֑כֶת) — "only going far, do not go far to go." The emphasis on distance reveals Pharaoh's anxiety: if they go too far, they might not come back. He is calculating the radius of control. The added imperative *ha'tiru ba'adi* (הַעְתִּ֖ירוּ בַּעֲדִֽי), "plead for me," is notable — Pharaoh asks for personal intercession. He wants the plague removed from himself specifically.

**v.29 — "Pharaoh must not deal deceptively again" (Heb. *al-yosef Par'oh hatel*, אַל־יֹסֵ֤ף פַּרְעֹה֙ הָתֵ֔ל):** Moses' warning is pointed. The verb *hatel* (from *talal*, תלל) means "to deceive, to mock, to deal treacherously." Moses names what Pharaoh has already done: he made a promise during the frog plague and broke it as soon as the pressure lifted. The accusation of deception frames Pharaoh not merely as stubborn but as dishonest — a treaty-breaker, a man whose word cannot be trusted. This is significant in the ancient Near Eastern context, where a king's word was considered binding. Moses is publicly challenging Pharaoh's honor.

**v.32 — "Pharaoh made his heart heavy this time also" (Heb. *vayyakhbed Par'oh et-libbo gam bapa'am hazzot*, וַיַּכְבֵּ֤ד פַּרְעֹה֙ אֶת־לִבּ֔וֹ גַּ֖ם בַּפַּ֣עַם הַזֹּ֑את):** The chapter ends as the frog plague ended: with *kaved*-hardening, Pharaoh as agent. The added phrase *gam bapa'am hazzot* — "this time also" — is the narrator's editorial commentary. The word *gam* ("also, even") and the demonstrative *hazzot* ("this") point backward to the frog episode (v.15) and draw a line of continuity. Pharaoh's pattern is now established: crisis yields concession, relief yields regression. "This time also" implies the narrator knows it will happen again. And the people are not released.

**The escalation pattern across three plagues in Exodus 8:**

The three plagues in this chapter form a carefully structured escalation:

1. **Frogs (vv. 1–15):** Magicians replicate the plague (adding to it). Pharaoh asks for intercession. Relief comes. Pharaoh breaks his word. *Kaved*-hardening (self-inflicted).

2. **Gnats (vv. 16–19):** No warning given. Magicians *fail* for the first time. They confess "the finger of God." Pharaoh ignores them. *Chazaq*-hardening (heart grows rigid).

3. **Swarms (vv. 20–32):** Warning at the water. First *distinction* between Israel and Egypt. Pharaoh begins negotiating. Moses accuses him of deception. *Kaved*-hardening (self-inflicted, "this time also").

The progression is clear: from replication to failure to irrelevance (the magicians disappear after the gnats). Simultaneously, from universal plague to distinguished plague (Goshen is protected). And from simple refusal to active negotiation and deception. The chapter traces Pharaoh's descent from defiance through desperation to dishonesty.

**Hardening verb tracker for Exodus 8:**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 8:15 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 8:19 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:32 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |

**Cumulative tracker (Exodus 7–8):**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 7:3 | *qashah* (קשה) | Hiphil | YHWH (announced) | "harden" |
| 7:13 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 7:14 | *kaved* (כבד) | Adjective | Description of state | "unyielding" |
| 7:22 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:15 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |
| 8:19 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 8:32 | *kaved* (כבד) | Hiphil | Pharaoh (self) | "made his heart heavy" |

Note: Through the first four plagues, every hardening is either self-described or self-inflicted. YHWH announced the *qashah*-hardening programmatically in 7:3, but it has not yet been enacted. Pharaoh is still the agent of his own resistance. This will change.

— *PNEUMA*
